pullRequest.title: Fix subscribe_on not forwarding scheduler to the source
pullRequest.body:
This PR fixes the `subscribe_on` operator that actually doesn't forward the scheduler to the source.

This is noticeable when one wants to implement an obsersvable by using a subscribe function:

```python
my_scheduler = EventLoopScheduler()

def source():
    def subscribe(observer, _scheduler):
        # here, _scheduler is None instead of my_scheduler
        ...
    return rx.create(subscribe)

source().pipe(
    ops.subscribe_on(my_scheduler)
).run()
```

I believe this is not aligned with the documentation which states the following:

> wrap the source sequence in order to run its subscription and unsubscription logic on the specified scheduler

I think it should fix #541 as well.

pullRequest.title: [Repo Assist] Fix timer resubscription: remove `nonlocal duetime` mutation (Closes #697)
pullRequest.body:
🤖 *This is an automated pull request from Repo Assist, an AI assistant for this repository.*

## Summary

Fixes `reactivex.timer(duetime, period)` incorrectly firing immediately on the second (and later) subscription when the same observable is reused — for example through `.pipe(ops.take(n), ops.repeat())`.

Closes #697

## Root Cause

`observable_timer_duetime_and_period` used `nonlocal duetime` inside its `subscribe` closure to convert a relative `duetime` (e.g. `10`) to an absolute `datetime`:

```python
nonlocal duetime                                    # ← captures outer variable
if not isinstance(duetime, datetime):
    duetime = _scheduler.now + _scheduler.to_timedelta(duetime)  # ← mutates it!
dt = duetime
```

Because `nonlocal` writes back to the enclosing function scope, the **first** subscription permanently overwrote `duetime` with the absolute time it computed (e.g. `datetime(tick=210)`). Every subsequent subscription skipped the conversion (it was already a `datetime`) and started its `dt` from that stale past time — causing `schedule_absolute` to fire immediately.

## Fix

Remove `nonlocal duetime`. Instead, compute a local `dt` per subscription:

```python
if not isinstance(duetime, datetime):
    dt: datetime = _scheduler.now + _scheduler.to_timedelta(duetime)
else:
    dt = duetime
```

`duetime` is now read-only inside the closure; each subscription independently computes its own start time relative to when it subscribes.

## Test

Added `test_periodic_timer_resubscription_respects_duetime` which reproduces the exact pattern from the issue report and asserts the correct emission times.

All existing timer tests continue to pass.

## Trade-offs

None — this is a pure bug fix with no API changes and no behavioral change for single-subscription usage.
